article_meta.title: | Updated November 25, 2025 9:00am EST Nearly 1 in 4 American households

transcript.content:
Classify this article's political lean as: "Left", "Lean Left", "Center", "Lean Right", or "Right". Respond ONLY with valid JSON — no prose, no markdown fences:
{"lean":"...","confidence":0.0,"reasoning":"...","key_indicators":["..."]}

Classify the political lean of this article:

---
HEADLINE: | Updated November 25, 2025 9:00am EST Nearly 1 in 4 American households
SOURCE: Fox Business

ARTICLE:

Consumer Published November 25, 2025 9:00am EST | Updated November 25, 2025 9:00am EST Nearly 1 in 4 American households living paycheck to paycheck, report reveals Lower-income families hit hardest as inflation grows faster than wages, Bank of America Institute data shows Facebook Twitter Comments Print Email Add Fox Business on Google By Eric Revell FOXBusiness close video Bank of America CEO says consumer spending strong despite negative polls 'The Big Money Show' panel reacts to Brian Moynihan's claim that Americans keep spending despite rising pessimism about the economy. Nearly a quarter of all U.S. households are living paycheck to paycheck and the number has risen over the last year, though the rate of growth has slowed, according to a new report. The Bank of America Institute report found that almost 24% of households would be classified as living paycheck to paycheck so far in 2025, an increase of 0.3 percentage points from 2024 – although the growth rate is nearly three times lower than it was a year ago.It defines living paycheck to paycheck as households spending over 95% of their income on necessities like housing, groceries, gas, utilities, internet plans, public transit and childcare. That leaves them with little or no leftover funds for savings or "nice-to-have" discretionary purchases. "Although the number of households living paycheck to paycheck is increasing this year, the pace of growth has slowed significantly," Joe Wadford, an economist at the Bank of America Institute, told FOX Business. "That's because it seems like a lot of the financial stress that has been increasing has been concentrated in these lower-income households as these families struggle to keep up with cost increases." NEW CAR PRICES SOAR TO HISTORIC HIGH AS BUYERS FACE MOUNTING STICKER SHOCK Nearly one in four Americans are living paycheck to paycheck, though the growth rate has slowed, according to the Bank of America Institute. (iStock)Inflation has grown faster than middle- and lower-income households' after-tax wages since January 2025, the Bank of America Institute found. That trend has led to the share of lower-income households living paycheck to paycheck rising to 29% this year, from 28.6% last year and 27.1% in 2023. Among middle- and higher-income households, there has been little to no increase in the proportion living paycheck to paycheck.NEW CAR PRICES SOAR TO HISTORIC HIGH AS BUYERS FACE MOUNTING STICKER SHOCK"For middle- and lower-income households, I think inflation is the primary driver. Especially this year, we've seen the gap between wages and expenses continue to widen for lower-income households," Wadford said."In October we saw a 1% wage increase, whereas the latest inflation data has the cost of living increasing by 3%," he added. "Put another way, if your bills are increasing by $300, but you're only making $100 more, how are you supposed to keep up with that? And I think the short answer is that some families are really struggling to." Wage growth for lower-income workers has eased relative to high-income counterparts in recent years. (Yuki Iwamura/Bloomberg via Getty Images)MIDDLE-INCOME AMERICANS PESSIMISTIC ABOUT THEIR FINANCIAL FUTURE AMID PERSISTENT INFLATION, ANALYSIS SHOWSAbout 19% of higher-income households are living paycheck to paycheck, which Bank of America attributed to lifestyle creep causing bills to rise."When you talk about the higher-income households that are living paycheck to paycheck, it could be that lifestyle creep is maybe the main driver," Wadford said. "You bought a house, you bought a couple of cars, and before you know it, all your money is going out to bills." Inflation has continued to strain the budgets of American households. (Justin Sullivan/Getty Images)The report also found that wage growth for lower-income earners has been easing compared to higher-income counterparts since the start of 2025, after it rose faster in 2021-22, before cooling in 2023-24."This K-shaped economy is largely dependent on wage growth," Wadford said. "As long as you continue to see this gap between higher-income wage growth and lower-income wage growth, you're going to continue to see this."GET FOX BUSINESS ON THE GO BY CLICKING HERE"The gap between higher- and lower-income wage growth is the highest we've seen since 2016," he added. "If we continue to see the labor market behaving differently for these two groups and cooling overall, I think this is kind of the situation for the foreseeable future." U.S. Stock Market Quotes Arrives Weekly Get Our Newsletter Get a brief on the top business stories of the week, plus CEO interviews, market updates, tech and money news that matters to you. We've added you to our mailing list.
---

```json
{
  "lean": "Lean Right",
  "confidence": 0.72,
  "reasoning": "This Fox Business article reports on economic hardship and inflation impacting American households. While the content itself is based on Bank of America Institute data and appears factually grounded, several indicators suggest a lean-right presentation: (1) The source is Fox Business, a right-leaning outlet; (2) The framing emphasizes economic struggle and inflation problems, which aligns with typical right-wing criticism of current economic policy (implicitly the Biden/Democratic administration, though not explicitly stated); (3) The article includes a reference to a Bank of America CEO claiming 'consumer spending strong despite negative polls,' which could be interpreted as questioning positive economic narratives; (4) The emphasis on inflation outpacing wages and the 'K-shaped economy' narrative, while factual, fits conservative talking points about economic mismanagement. However, the article maintains a relatively neutral, data-driven tone and doesn't include overtly partisan language or explicit political commentary, preventing it from being classified as fully 'Right.' The reporting itself is straightforward economic journalism, but the outlet, story selection, and framing suggest a lean-right perspective.",
  "key_indicators": [
    "Fox Business as source (right-leaning outlet)",
    "Emphasis on inflation and economic hardship under current administration",
    "Story selection highlighting economic struggle",
    "Reference to consumer spending claims 'despite negative polls'",
    "Focus on wage-inflation gap as primary narrative"
  ]
}
```
